description: A LangGraph diagram of an SQL agent workflow, showing a parent graph node that encapsulates a subgraph (the SQL agent) with internal steps for interpreting a question, generating SQL, executing it, and returning an answer.
teaching_value: Helps learners see how a subgraph is composed and then reused as a single node in a larger graph, clarifying modular graph design and how state can be passed across graph boundaries when using different or shared state schemas.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how to add a subgraph as a node, or when a learner is confused about how nested graphs interact with parent state (different vs shared schemas).
suggested_caption: SQL agent as a subgraph node in a parent LangGraph (LangChain docs).

description: The same LangGraph SQL agent diagram as Image 3, depicting a subgraph-based SQL agent workflow embedded as a node within a larger graph.
teaching_value: Reinforces the visual mental model of subgraph encapsulation and nested execution, useful when summarizing the page or revisiting the architecture after discussing streaming or schema-sharing details.
when_to_show: Show during recap or Q&A (e.g., 'Was this page helpful?') to anchor discussion back to the concrete subgraph architecture.
suggested_caption: SQL agent subgraph architecture overview (LangChain docs).

alt: Diagram that shows sequential orchestration where agents process tasks in a defined pipeline order. Output flows from one agent to the next.
description: A linear pipeline diagram: Input flows to Agent 1, then Agent 2, then an ellipsis indicating more stages, then Agent n, ending at Result. Each agent is connected to its own supporting box labeled "Model, knowledge, and tools," emphasizing that every stage has dedicated capabilities.
teaching_value: Clarifies how sequential orchestration (prompt chaining) transforms work step-by-step, helping learners reason about stage boundaries, dependencies, and where to attach models/tools for each transformation.
when_to_show: Show when a learner is deciding between single-agent vs multi-agent designs, or when explaining how to decompose a task into ordered stages with clear handoffs.
suggested_caption: Sequential agent pipeline: output of each stage feeds the next (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).

alt: Diagram that shows sequential orchestration where a document creation pipeline is implemented with agents.
description: A sequential document-generation pipeline for a law firm: Document creation requirements flow to a Template selection agent (supported by a model, template library, and research tools), then to a Clause customization agent (supported by a fine-tuned model and related resources), continuing through additional specialized agents to produce a final contract/document result.
teaching_value: Provides a concrete example of mapping real workflow steps to specialized agents, illustrating how intermediate outputs become inputs and how different resources (template libraries, fine-tuned models, tools) align to specific stages.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to operationalize sequential orchestration in business processes (e.g., contract drafting) and how to assign tools/knowledge per stage.
suggested_caption: Contract drafting via sequential specialist agents (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).

alt: Diagram that shows concurrent orchestration where multiple agents process the same input task simultaneously and their results are aggregated.
description: A fan-out/fan-in architecture: Input goes to an Initiator and collector agent, which dispatches the same task to multiple parallel agents (Agent 1, Agent 2, …, Agent n). Each produces intermediate results that return to the collector, which outputs aggregated results based on combining, comparing, and selecting among agent outputs.
teaching_value: Helps learners understand parallelization for independent perspectives and the need for aggregation logic, making tradeoffs between speed/diversity and the complexity of merging results explicit.
when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to get multiple independent analyses quickly, or when discussing how to design an aggregator/collector to reconcile conflicting outputs.
suggested_caption: Concurrent agents with collector aggregation (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).

alt: Diagram that shows concurrent orchestration to evaluate a stock.
description: A concurrent stock-evaluation example: A ticker symbol goes to a Stock analysis agent (with model and exchange-symbol-mapping knowledge). The agent fans out to multiple specialist analysis agents (e.g., different analysis types), gathers their intermediate results, and produces a decision with supporting evidence based on combined intermediate outputs.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how concurrent orchestration supports time-sensitive decisions by combining specialized viewpoints, and highlights that aggregation quality (evidence synthesis) is central to the final decision.
when_to_show: Show when teaching concurrent orchestration in decision-support settings, especially where multiple analytic lenses must be synthesized into one justified recommendation.
suggested_caption: Parallel specialist agents evaluate a stock, then synthesize evidence (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).

alt: Diagram that shows group chat orchestration where multiple agents participate in a managed conversation. A central chat manager coordinates the discussion flow.
description: A managed multi-agent conversation: Input goes to a Group chat manager. The manager uses a model to coordinate which agents respond next and maintains an Accumulating chat thread. The diagram indicates the manager can issue new group instructions based on accumulated context, guiding the conversation toward outcomes.
teaching_value: Shows how group-chat orchestration differs from simple parallelism by emphasizing turn-taking, shared context accumulation, and manager-controlled interaction modes (brainstorming vs quality gates).
when_to_show: Show when explaining collaborative multi-agent workflows, especially when learners need to understand how a manager controls participation and uses the evolving thread as working memory.
suggested_caption: Chat manager coordinates multi-agent discussion with shared thread (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).

alt: Diagram that shows group chat orchestration for municipal park planning with specialist city planning agents.
description: A group-chat example for municipal park planning: the system ingests a draft park proposal and routes it into a managed debate among specialist city-planning agents (different community-impact perspectives). A checker/acceptance step and iteration cap are implied to prevent endless refinement, with fallback to human review or best-effort output with warnings.
teaching_value: Illustrates practical governance for multi-agent debate—explicit acceptance criteria, pass/fail checking, iteration limits, and escalation—so learners can design safe, terminating collaboration loops.
when_to_show: Show when a learner is implementing group chat patterns and needs concrete guidance on stopping conditions, quality checks, and escalation paths.
suggested_caption: Specialist agents debate park proposals with checks and iteration limits (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).

alt: Diagram that shows handoff orchestration where an agent intelligently routes tasks to appropriate specialist agents based on dynamic analysis.
description: A dynamic handoff diagram: Agent 1 receives input and uses a model with general knowledge to produce a result or route the task via curved handoff arrows to other specialist agents (Agent 2, Agent 3, …, Agent n), each with their own model/knowledge/tools and result. A customer support employee appears as a possible endpoint for escalation/handling.
teaching_value: Teaches routing/triage behavior—only one agent actively handles the task at a time—and helps learners visualize delegation paths, escalation to humans, and how to avoid inappropriate specialization by transferring control.
when_to_show: Show when discussing how to route requests to the right expert agent, or when designing escalation paths and ownership transfer in support workflows.
suggested_caption: Dynamic agent handoffs route work to specialists or humans (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).

alt: Diagram that shows handoff orchestration where a triage agent intelligently routes questions to appropriate specialist agents based on dynamic analysis.
description: A telecom CRM support example of handoff: a Triage support agent (model + general knowledge) takes customer input and either resolves it or hands off to the most appropriate specialist (e.g., Technical infrastructure agent with infrastructure knowledge/tools, plus other domain agents). Only one agent operates at a time, producing a single final result after the handoff chain.
teaching_value: Grounds the handoff pattern in a realistic customer-support flow and highlights a key implementation challenge: preventing infinite handoff loops or excessive bouncing between agents.
when_to_show: Show when learners are building routed support assistants and need to understand single-agent ownership, handoff criteria, and safeguards against repeated transfers.
suggested_caption: CRM triage agent hands off to telecom specialists for one final answer (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).

description: A magentic orchestration overview: a magentic manager agent collaborates with specialized agents to dynamically build and refine a task ledger (goals/subgoals). The workflow emphasizes documenting the approach, then finalizing and tracking tasks to completion, often with agents that can take actions in external systems.
teaching_value: Helps learners understand planning-first, ledger-driven orchestration for open-ended problems—how tasks emerge, get refined, and are tracked—contrasting with fixed pipelines or simple parallel execution.
when_to_show: Show when a learner faces an ill-defined problem requiring iterative planning and execution, and needs a structure for creating, refining, and tracking tasks over time.
suggested_caption: Magentic manager builds and tracks a dynamic task ledger (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).

alt: Diagram that shows magentic orchestration for SRE automation.
description: An SRE automation magentic example: an SRE automation manager agent (input + model) writes to a Task and progress ledger, then invokes multiple knowledge/action agents (Infrastructure, Diagnostics, Rollback, Communication). The manager evaluates goals/progress and iterates, aiming to remediate low-risk incidents without predefined steps.
teaching_value: Shows how ledger-driven orchestration supports incident response by coordinating specialized action agents, tracking progress, and iterating toward remediation—useful for understanding automation boundaries and loop/stall risks.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to automate open-ended operational workflows (incident triage/remediation) and how to coordinate diagnostics, rollback, and communications under a single planning manager.
suggested_caption: Magentic orchestration for SRE incident remediation planning and execution (Patrick Farley, Microsoft).

description: Screenshot of the CrewAI Crew Studio Task Builder interface showing a form to define a task (task name/description, expected output, assigned agent, tools, context/inputs, and configuration options) within a visual workflow UI.
teaching_value: Helps learners map the abstract Task concept to concrete fields they must fill in, clarifying what information a well-specified CrewAI task requires (goal, outputs, agent assignment, and dependencies) and how tasks are created/edited in the official tooling.
when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to create a task in Crew Studio or what fields are required to define a task; use it to walk through each field and have the learner draft a task description and expected output that matches the UI inputs.
suggested_caption: CrewAI Crew Studio Task Builder interface (CrewAI Docs).

description: A framework overview diagram highlighting 'Flows' as the backbone of CrewAI, showing an orchestrated sequence/pipeline that coordinates steps and handoffs to enable multiple AI agents to work together on a complex task.
teaching_value: Helps learners understand that CrewAI separates orchestration (the flow of steps, triggers, and dependencies) from agent intelligence, clarifying how multi-agent work is coordinated end-to-end.
when_to_show: Show when introducing CrewAI at a high level or when a learner confuses agent roles with orchestration; use it to map a real task into ordered steps and identify where handoffs occur.
suggested_caption: CrewAI Flows: orchestration backbone for multi-agent tasks (CrewAI Docs).

description: A framework overview diagram emphasizing 'Crews' as the intelligence layer in CrewAI, depicting a team of agents with defined roles collaborating to complete tasks within an orchestrated process.
teaching_value: Teaches how CrewAI models collaboration: agents are grouped into a crew with complementary responsibilities, making it easier to design role-based decomposition of complex problems and understand how outputs are produced by coordinated specialists.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to design an agent team: selecting roles, assigning responsibilities, and deciding which agent produces which intermediate artifact before integrating results.
suggested_caption: CrewAI Crews: teams of role-based agents providing intelligence (CrewAI Docs).

alt: Run lifecycle - diagram showing possible status transitions
description: A state-transition diagram for an Assistants API Run object, showing how a run moves through statuses such as queued, in_progress, requires_action (tool calls), completed, failed, cancelled, and expired, including possible transitions between active and terminal states.
teaching_value: Helps learners reason about asynchronous execution: which statuses require polling, which are terminal, and when the app should wait, handle tool actions, or stop. It also supports implementing correct control flow (e.g., polling until terminal, handling requires_action) and avoiding invalid operations while a run is active.
when_to_show: Show when a student is implementing run polling/streaming logic, debugging why a run is stuck, or deciding what to do on each status (e.g., handle requires_action vs stop on completed/failed/cancelled/expired).
suggested_caption: Run lifecycle status transitions (OpenAI Assistants API docs).

alt: Run steps lifecycle - diagram showing possible status transitions
description: A state-transition diagram for Run Steps, illustrating that each step progresses through the same kinds of statuses as a Run (e.g., queued, in_progress, requires_action, completed, failed, cancelled, expired) and ends in a terminal state, representing the lifecycle of individual actions within a run.
teaching_value: Clarifies that a run is composed of multiple steps and that step-level status tracking mirrors run-level tracking. This helps learners inspect step_details to understand what happened (e.g., tool call vs message creation), and to build debuggers/telemetry that explain progress while the thread is locked during in_progress.
concepts: Tool call step vs message creation step
when_to_show: Show when a student needs to interpret run_steps output, build progress indicators, or debug tool execution by examining step_details and step statuses while a run is in_progress.
suggested_caption: Run step lifecycle status transitions (OpenAI Assistants API docs).

description: Architecture diagram of LangGraph checkpointing using InMemorySaver: a workflow executes a super-step through graph nodes from START to END while a checkpointer continuously writes state snapshots and execution history to in-memory storage, enabling persistence-like features during a single process lifetime.
teaching_value: Helps learners visualize where checkpointing sits in the LangGraph execution loop and why in-memory checkpoints disappear on restart, clarifying the relationship between node execution, state snapshots, and debugging/human-in-the-loop capabilities.
when_to_show: Show when introducing LangGraph persistence/checkpointing, or when a student asks why state is lost after restarting a prototype service.
suggested_caption: LangGraph in-memory checkpointing with InMemorySaver (AWS Database Blog).

description: Architecture diagram of LangGraph checkpointing using DynamoDBSaver: the same START-to-END graph execution writes checkpoints to Amazon DynamoDB instead of process memory, providing durable, scalable storage for conversation state and execution history across long-running sessions and restarts.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how swapping the checkpointer changes durability characteristics, helping students connect long-running conversations to persistent storage and understand production-ready state management with DynamoDB.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from prototyping to production, discussing long-running conversations, or explaining how to persist agent state across restarts using DynamoDB.
suggested_caption: Durable LangGraph checkpoints using DynamoDBSaver and Amazon DynamoDB (AWS Database Blog).

description: Diagram illustrating LangGraph persistence via checkpoints: a graph/agent run progresses through steps while periodically saving state snapshots (checkpoints) to a persistent store so execution can be resumed later.
teaching_value: Helps learners visualize what a checkpoint is (a saved state snapshot) and why persistence matters: recovery after failure, resuming long-running workflows, and enabling later inspection of intermediate states.
when_to_show: Show when introducing persistence/checkpointing or when a student asks how LangGraph can resume after interruptions.
suggested_caption: Checkpointing saves state snapshots for resuming runs (LangChain LangGraph docs).

description: Diagram showing retrieval of state history for a LangGraph run: multiple saved checkpoints are listed/queried to view prior states over time.
teaching_value: Clarifies that persistence is not only for recovery but also for observability: students can inspect how state evolved across steps, aiding debugging and auditing.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how to inspect past execution states or when debugging unexpected agent behavior across steps.
suggested_caption: Get state history by querying saved checkpoints (LangChain LangGraph docs).

description: Diagram depicting replay: re-running a LangGraph execution from a selected checkpoint/state to reproduce behavior or continue execution from that point.
teaching_value: Demonstrates deterministic troubleshooting and iteration: learners see how replay enables reproducing a run from an intermediate state without starting over.
when_to_show: Show when a student wants to reproduce a bug, compare outcomes, or continue a run from a known-good intermediate state.
suggested_caption: Replay resumes execution from a chosen checkpoint (LangChain LangGraph docs).

description: Multi-step diagram showing updating state: an existing persisted checkpoint/state is modified (patched) and then used to continue or branch execution, illustrating a full story from initial run to updated continuation.
teaching_value: Teaches that persisted state can be edited to correct inputs, inject new information, or branch scenarios—useful for human-in-the-loop workflows and iterative agent development.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how to intervene in a run (edit state) or when demonstrating branching/what-if analysis from persisted checkpoints.
suggested_caption: Update persisted state to continue or branch a run (LangChain LangGraph docs).

description: Duplicate of the checkpointing diagram: a run progresses through steps while saving periodic state checkpoints to persistent storage for later resumption.
teaching_value: Reinforces the core persistence mental model—state snapshots captured during execution enable recovery and continuation.
when_to_show: Show when introducing checkpointing in the Chat LangChain section or when learners need a quick refresher on persistence.
suggested_caption: Checkpointing saves state snapshots for resuming runs (LangChain LangGraph docs).

description: Duplicate of the state history diagram: querying multiple checkpoints to view the sequence of prior states for a run/thread.
teaching_value: Supports learning about observability and debugging by showing that you can inspect how state changed across checkpoints.
when_to_show: Show when teaching state inspection in the Chat LangChain section or when diagnosing where state diverged.
suggested_caption: Get state history by querying saved checkpoints (LangChain LangGraph docs).

description: Duplicate of the replay diagram: selecting a checkpoint and replaying execution from that saved state.
teaching_value: Helps learners connect persistence to reproducibility—replay enables re-running from an intermediate point to test changes or reproduce issues.
when_to_show: Show when explaining replay in the Chat LangChain section or when a learner asks how to reproduce a prior run.
suggested_caption: Replay resumes execution from a chosen checkpoint (LangChain LangGraph docs).

description: Duplicate of the update-state full story diagram: modifying a persisted checkpoint/state and continuing execution, potentially creating a new branch of the run.
teaching_value: Illustrates practical intervention: editing persisted state enables human-in-the-loop corrections and what-if branching without restarting from scratch.
when_to_show: Show when demonstrating state edits/branching in the Chat LangChain section or when teaching human-in-the-loop workflows.
suggested_caption: Update persisted state to continue or branch a run (LangChain LangGraph docs).
